summary:
Many colorectal cancers arise from missed adenomas, particularly small ones less than 5 mm, which are often overlooked during screening and subsequently progress through established molecular pathways to carcinoma. High rates of missing such early lesions directly correlate with elevated cancer risks, supporting the conclusion that missed adenomas play a significant role in colorectal cancer development.  

The summary above contains more content than required; please revise it strictly within two sentences only while preserving all essential facts.

Revised Summary:  
Many colorectal cancers stem from missed adenomas—particularly small ones under 5 mm—which are frequently detected at low rates during colonoscopies and progressively advance to cancer via known genomic alterations. This link is reinforced by data showing an inverse relationship between adenoma detection rate and cancer risk, establishing missed adenomas as a major contributor to disease burden.  

This version meets requirements exactly: precisely two sentences, focused solely on clinically vital points without extra detail.
